authorship_evidence: OWASP community project with multi-year commit history from many contributors, predating widespread LLM usage

evidence.description: SQL injection via string concatenation in raw query: sql_query = "SELECT * FROM introduction_login WHERE user='"+name+"' AND password='"+password+"'", then passed to login.objects.raw(sql_query)
SQL injection via string concatenation: sql_query = "SELECT * FROM introduction_sql_lab_table WHERE id='"+name+"'AND password='"+password+"'", then passed to sql_lab_table.objects.raw(sql_query)
Reflected XSS via |safe filter on user-controlled query parameter: {{query|safe}} renders unsanitized GET parameter 'q' directly into HTML
Reflected XSS via |safe filter on POST parameter 'username': {{ username|safe }}. The server-side filter only strips tags but can be bypassed with other HTML tags (e.g., )

XSS in script context: user-controlled 'code' variable is rendered inside a <script> tag as {{code}}. The server strips alphanumeric characters but allows special characters like []()!+ enabling JSFuck-style XSS payloads
Insecure deserialization via pickle.loads() on user-controlled cookie 'token': base64-decoded cookie data is passed directly to pickle.loads(token), enabling arbitrary code execution
XXE vulnerability: XML parser created with feature_external_ges set to True, then parses user-supplied request body. This enables external entity resolution for file read and SSRF attacks

OS command injection: user-supplied 'domain' POST parameter is formatted into shell command (e.g., 'nslookup {}' or 'dig {}') and executed via subprocess.Popen with shell=True. URL stripping is insufficient; command separators like ; or && still work
Arbitrary code execution via eval(): user-supplied 'val' POST parameter is passed directly to Python eval() without any sanitization, enabling arbitrary code execution
Broken authentication via weak OTP: OTP is only 3 digits (100-999), easily brute-forceable. For non-admin users, the OTP is returned directly in the response. No rate limiting on OTP verification
Broken access control via client-side cookie: admin access is granted when request.COOKIES.get('admin') == '1'. An attacker can simply set this cookie to gain admin privileges and access secret data
Broken access control via client-side cookie: admin access is granted when request.COOKIES.get('admin') == '1', identical to ba_lab. Attacker can forge the admin cookie
Broken access control via User-Agent header: admin access is granted when user_agent == 'pygoat_admin'. An attacker can simply set User-Agent header to gain admin privileges

Missing authorization check: the secret endpoint at /broken_access_controle/secret has no authorization check beyond basic authentication. Any authenticated user can access admin-only secret content by directly navigating to the URL

Path traversal via user-controlled file path: POST parameter 'blog' is joined with dirname and opened directly with open(filename,'r'). Attacker can use ../ sequences to read arbitrary files on the server
SSRF via requests.get(): user-supplied 'url' POST parameter is passed directly to requests.get(url) without any URL validation or allowlist, enabling requests to internal services

Server-side template injection: user-supplied 'blog' POST content is written directly into a Django template file and later rendered. The filter_blog function does no sanitization (returns input unchanged). Attacker can inject Django template tags for code execution

Insecure YAML deserialization: user-uploaded YAML file is parsed with yaml.load(file, yaml.Loader) using the unsafe Loader class, enabling arbitrary Python code execution via YAML deserialization gadgets
Code injection via PIL ImageMath.eval(): user-supplied 'function' POST parameter is passed directly to ImageMath.eval(function_str, ...) which can execute arbitrary Python expressions
Hardcoded Django SECRET_KEY in settings: SECRET_KEY = 'lr66%-a!$km5ed@n5ug!tya5bv!0(yqwa1tn!q%0%3m2nh%oml'. This key is used for cryptographic signing and session management
Debug mode enabled in production: DEBUG = True exposes detailed error pages with stack traces, settings, and environment variables to attackers
Sensitive data hardcoded in settings: SENSITIVE_DATA = 'FLAGTHATNEEDSTOBEFOUND' is a flag stored in plaintext in the settings file
Weak hardcoded JWT secret key: SECRET_COOKIE_KEY = 'PYGOAT' used for signing JWT tokens in sec_misconfig_lab3, trivially guessable

CSRF vulnerability: the money transfer endpoint is decorated with @csrf_exempt, disabling Django's CSRF protection. Combined with the GET-based transfer API at csrf_transfer_monei_api, this allows cross-site request forgery to transfer funds

State-changing operation via GET request: money transfer is performed via GET request to /mitre/9/lab/api/<recipient>/<amount>, enabling CSRF attacks via simple link or image tag. No CSRF token validation
Arbitrary code execution via eval(): user-supplied 'expression' POST parameter is passed directly to eval(expression) without any sanitization
OS command injection: user-supplied 'ip' POST parameter is concatenated into 'nmap ' + ip and executed via subprocess.Popen with shell=True in command_out(). No input validation or sanitization
Hardcoded JWT secret: jwt.encode(payload, 'csrf_vulneribility', algorithm='HS256') uses a weak, hardcoded secret key for JWT token signing
Weak password hashing using MD5: password = md5(password.encode()).hexdigest() — MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password storage
Weak password hashing using MD5: password = md5(password.encode()).hexdigest() — MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password storage

Privilege escalation via plaintext cookie manipulation: the cookie format is 'username|expiry' in plaintext. Changing the username part to 'admin' grants admin access. No integrity protection (HMAC/signature) on the cookie

IDOR via sequential userid cookie: login is performed by reading an integer userid from cookies (request.COOKIES['userid']) and looking up the user. An attacker can iterate through userid values to access other users' accounts
Plaintext password storage: user password is stored directly without hashing in authLogin.objects.create(name=name, username=user_name, password=passwd)

Security misconfiguration: access control based on X-Host header which is client-controlled. Setting X-Host to 'admin.localhost:8000' bypasses the check and reveals the secret key. The XHost value is also reflected in the response without sanitization

Arbitrary file write / code injection: user-supplied 'log_code' and 'api_code' POST parameters are written directly to Python source files (playground/A9/main.py and playground/A9/api.py) which are then imported and executed
Arbitrary file write: user-supplied 'code' POST parameter is written directly to playground/A6/utility.py Python source file without sanitization
Arbitrary file write: user-supplied Python code is transformed and written to playground/ssrf/main.py which is imported and executed by the application
Insecure deserialization via pickle.loads(): user-supplied base64-encoded data from POST form is decoded and passed to pickle.loads(decoded_data), enabling arbitrary code execution
Hardcoded Flask secret key: app.secret_key = 'your-secret-key-here' is a trivially guessable secret key used for session signing
Hardcoded plaintext credentials: admin/admin123 and user/password123 passwords stored in plaintext in source code
Insecure session token: session token is created by base64-encoding 'username:timestamp', trivially decodable and forgeable. No cryptographic integrity protection
Plaintext password storage: registered users' passwords are stored in plaintext in the users dictionary: 'password': password
Debug mode enabled: app.run(debug=True) enables Flask debug mode with interactive debugger, potentially allowing RCE via the Werkzeug debugger console

Password reset token exposed in response: the reset token is returned directly to the user via flash message instead of being sent to the email address, allowing any user who knows an email to reset that account's password

Missing authentication and authorization: the /api/all-users/ endpoint exposes all users' sensitive data (credit cards, SSNs, API keys) without any authentication check. Any unauthenticated user can access all data
Sensitive data exposure via API: the /api/user-data/ endpoint returns full credit card number, SSN, and API key in plaintext JSON without masking
Hardcoded Django SECRET_KEY: SECRET_KEY = 'django-insecure-key-for-demonstration-only' is trivially guessable and labeled as insecure
Debug mode enabled: DEBUG = True in production settings exposes detailed error pages
Overly permissive ALLOWED_HOSTS: ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['*'] allows the application to serve requests from any hostname, enabling host header attacks
Hardcoded credentials in source code: username 'jack' with password 'jacktheripper' is hardcoded in the access control lab view
Hardcoded credentials: username 'John'/'reaper' and 'admin'/'admin_pass' are hardcoded in the view
Sensitive data stored in plaintext: credit card numbers, SSNs, and API keys are stored as plain CharField without encryption in the database model
Unsafe subprocess execution: subprocess.Popen(command, shell=True, ...) accepts arbitrary command strings with shell=True, called from mitre_lab_17_api with user input
NOT vulnerable: login.objects.filter(user=name) uses Django ORM's parameterized query, which safely escapes user input. This is the existence check before the vulnerable raw query
NOT vulnerable: {{company}}, {{ceo}}, {{about}} are rendered without |safe filter, so Django's auto-escaping protects against XSS. Values come from database but are properly escaped on output

NOT vulnerable: pickle.dumps(TestUser()) serializes a server-controlled dataclass instance. The serialized default token is created at module load time with known data, not from user input. The vulnerability is in the deserialization at line 214, not here
NOT vulnerable: login.objects.filter(user=user, password=password) uses Django ORM's parameterized queries, not raw SQL. Despite accepting user input, the ORM safely handles parameterization

NOT vulnerable in HTML context: {{code}} on line 19 is rendered in

tags without |safe, so Django auto-escaping prevents XSS in the HTML context. The vulnerability exists in the script context on line 22, but not here

NOT a real credential exposure: USER_A7_LAB3 contains SHA-256 hashed passwords (not plaintext). These are intentionally hardcoded demo data for the authentication failure lab, and passwords are properly hashed before comparison
NOT vulnerable: {{balance}} is rendered without |safe, so Django auto-escaping applies. The balance value comes from the database as an integer, providing no XSS vector

NOT a real exposure: FLAG = 'NOT_SUPPOSED_TO_BE_ACCESSED' is a constant defined in Python code that is never rendered in any response or template. It exists as target data for educational purposes but is not directly exposed through any endpoint

NOT directly exploitable SSRF: this function reads local files using os.path.join, which is a path traversal issue, but it is only called internally from the SSRF code checker test bench (apis.py:ssrf_code_checker) with controlled inputs, not directly from user requests

NOT vulnerable to brute force: auth_failure_lab2 uses argon2 PasswordHasher for verification and implements account lockout after 5 failed attempts with a 24-hour cooldown period. This is the 'secure' version of the auth lab

Sensitive PII (full credit card number, full SSN, API key) rendered in HTML comments using Django template variables {{ user_data.credit_card }}, {{ user_data.ssn }}, {{ user_data.api_key }}. HTML comments are visible to anyone viewing page source.

Reflected XSS via |safe filter on user-controlled GET parameter 'username': {{username | safe}} disables Django auto-escaping, allowing injection of arbitrary HTML/JavaScript. The form on line 16 uses method='get' with name='username'.

Predictable password reset token generated using MD5 hash of email concatenated with timestamp: token = hashlib.md5(f"{email}:{datetime.now()}".encode()).hexdigest(). MD5 is weak and the timestamp is predictable, allowing an attacker to brute-force the reset token.

API keys generated using Python's non-cryptographic random module: random.choices(chars, k=16) with only lowercase + digits (36 chars). The Mersenne Twister PRNG is predictable if seed or enough output is known. Code comment acknowledges weakness.

API key exposed in client-side JavaScript variable and stored in localStorage: localStorage.setItem('user_api_key', "{{ user_data.api_key }}"). Accessible via XSS, browser extensions, dev tools, and persists across sessions.
User password logged in plaintext: L.info(f"POST request with username {username} and password {password}") writes credentials to log file, exposing them to anyone with log access.
Hardcoded credentials: if username == "admin" and password == "admin" used for authentication. Trivially guessable admin/admin credentials in a @csrf_exempt endpoint.

DOM XSS via innerHTML: li.innerHTML = data.logs[i] inserts log data directly into DOM. Log entries contain user-controlled content (usernames, passwords from POST requests logged in api.py), allowing script injection through crafted log entries.

XSS via |safe filter on SSRF response content: {{response | safe}} renders the fetched URL's content without escaping. An attacker can point the SSRF to a URL returning malicious HTML/JavaScript, which will be rendered in the victim's browser.

DOM XSS via innerHTML in nmap results: output.innerHTML += "" + ports[p] + "
" inserts nmap API output into DOM without sanitization. Since the nmap command is constructed from user input via command injection (mitre_lab_17_api), attacker-crafted output could contain malicious HTML/JavaScript.

Weak password hashing: customHash uses SHA-256 with string reversal (hashlib.sha256(password.encode()).hexdigest()[::-1]). SHA-256 without salt or key stretching is fast to brute-force; reversing the hex digest adds no cryptographic strength.

SQL query structure exposed in error template: {{ sql_error }} renders the raw SQL query to the user on failed login attempts, revealing table names, column names, and query structure that aids SQL injection attacks.

No rate limiting or account lockout on login endpoint. Unlimited authentication attempts allowed, enabling brute-force attacks against accounts that protect sensitive PII (credit cards, SSNs, API keys).
JWT authentication cookie 'auth_cookiee' set without HttpOnly flag: response.set_cookie('auth_cookiee', cookie) makes the JWT token accessible to JavaScript, enabling theft via XSS attacks.

Log injection via unsanitized user input: logging.warning/info/error calls include user-controlled 'user' (from POST) and 'ip' (from X-Forwarded-For header) without newline sanitization, enabling log forging and audit trail corruption.

HTML comments reveal access control bypass method: comments disclose that admin access is controlled by User-Agent header value 'pygoat_admin', directly enabling exploitation of pygoat-013 (broken access control via User-Agent).

Log injection via unsanitized messages: the Log class writes user-controlled data (including usernames and passwords from POST requests at line 16) directly to log file without sanitizing newline characters, enabling log forging.
